did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used the k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN) algorithm, a supervised machine learning method, to classify dermoscopic images of moles into melanoma, non-melanoma, and unknown categories. The system extracted geometrical features (greatest/shortest diameter, equivalent diameter, irregularity index) based on the ABCD-rule and achieved an overall accuracy of 86.67%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a skin cancer detection system using the k-Nearest Neighbors algorithm on a Raspberry Pi. It details specific methodology (image processing, feature extraction from ISIC Archive), implementation, and results (86.67% accuracy on 15 test samples), which characterizes it as an original technical study rather than a review.

title: Skin Cancer Detection and Classification for Moles Using K-Nearest Neighbor Algorithm
brief_summary: This study developed a portable skin cancer detection and classification system for moles using the k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN) algorithm implemented on a Raspberry Pi. The system extracted geometrical features—greatest and shortest diameter, equivalent diameter, and irregularity index—based on the ABCD-rule of dermoscopy from dermoscopic images sourced from the ISIC Archive. The dataset consisted of 45 samples (15 each for melanoma, non-melanoma, and unknown), with 15 samples used for testing. The system achieved an overall classification accuracy of 86.67% on the test set. The results demonstrate the feasibility of using low-cost, portable hardware for automated skin cancer diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three primary diagnostic classes used in the classification task. The dataset contained 15 melanoma samples (9 in the test set). The system achieved 86.67% overall accuracy in predicting this class.

One of the three primary diagnostic classes used in the classification task. This category encompasses basal cell carcinoma and squamous cell carcinoma as mentioned in the introduction. The dataset contained 15 non-melanoma samples (5 in the test set).

One of the three primary diagnostic classes used in the classification task. The dataset contained 15 unknown samples (1 in the test set). This class was included in the k-NN classification alongside melanoma and non-melanoma.
Mentioned in the Introduction as a common type of skin cancer and categorized under non-melanoma, but not explicitly used as a distinct label in the experimental classification task.
Mentioned in the Introduction as a common type of skin cancer and categorized under non-melanoma, but not explicitly used as a distinct label in the experimental classification task.

all_task_types.target: Diagnostic category of the mole (Melanoma, Non-Melanoma, or Unknown)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs a three-class classification task using the k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN) algorithm to predict whether a dermoscopic image of a mole belongs to the classes 'Melanoma', 'Non-Melanoma', or 'Unknown'. The system achieved an overall accuracy of 86.67% on a test set of 15 samples.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive
datasets_used.description: Dermoscopic images of skin lesions sourced from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) Archive via internet resources. The images were manually acquired by the researchers and labeled according to their diagnosis from the archive.

use.training: Used for training the k-Nearest Neighbors classifier; 30 samples were used for training (45 total minus 15 testing).
use.testing: Used for testing the system's accuracy; 15 samples were used (9 Melanoma, 5 Non-Melanoma, 1 Unknown).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It reported classification accuracy (86.67%) and a confusion matrix but did not perform statistical significance testing.

ISIC Archive.notes: The system achieved an overall accuracy of 86.67% on the test set. The dataset consisted of 45 total samples (15 per class), with 15 used for testing. The k-NN algorithm was implemented on a Raspberry Pi 3.

architecture_used.input_size: Resized to fit the required number of pixels (specific dimensions not reported); images converted to grayscale

architecture_details.feature_extraction_method: Geometrical features based on the ABCD-rule of Dermoscopy
image_processing_pipeline: Color space transformation (RGB to Grayscale using COLOR_RGB2GRAY)

preprocessing_techniques: Image resizing to fit device capability and required pixel dimensions
Contrast and brightness modification to compensate for non-uniform illumination
Image blurring using GaussianBlur command (Gaussian kernel convolution) to reduce noise
Color space transformation from RGB to Grayscale using COLOR_RGB2GRAY function
Dilation to enlarge boundaries of foreground pixels
Thresholding using TRESH_BINARY_INV function to convert grayscale images to binary
Edge detection to identify boundaries and discontinuities
Artifact removal (fine hairs, noise, and air bubbles)
conclusions: The study successfully developed a portable skin cancer detection and classification system for moles using the k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN) algorithm implemented on a Raspberry Pi single-board computer.
The system achieved an overall classification accuracy of 86.67% on a test set of 15 dermoscopic images sourced from the ISIC Archive.

The classification was based on geometrical features extracted according to the ABCD-rule of dermoscopy, specifically greatest diameter, shortest diameter, equivalent diameter, and irregularity index.
The system was able to distinguish between three classes: melanoma, non-melanoma, and unknown.
The study acknowledges limitations regarding the small sample size (45 total samples, 15 testing) due to availability in the archive and the restriction of test samples to human subjects.
The current system requires manual image acquisition via internet resources rather than direct capture, as it lacks an integrated camera.

Future development is suggested to include integration of a sophisticated camera to directly capture mole images, which would eliminate the need for a separate dermatoscope and potentially reduce production costs.
important_remarks: The study suffers from a critically small sample size, using only 45 total samples (15 per class) with just 15 samples in the test set, which limits statistical power and generalizability.
The dataset is heavily imbalanced in the test set (9 Melanoma, 5 Non-Melanoma, 1 Unknown), making the accuracy metric potentially misleading for the 'Unknown' class.
The system lacks direct image acquisition capabilities, requiring manual image acquisition from the ISIC Archive via internet resources, which hinders real-time clinical deployment.
The 'k' hyperparameter for the k-Nearest Neighbors algorithm is not reported in the text.
No external validation was performed; the system was tested only on data from the ISIC Archive.
The study did not report any statistical significance tests, confidence intervals, or p-values to support the 86.67% accuracy claim.
The test samples were limited exclusively to human subjects, excluding potential animal or synthetic data validation.

The system relies on geometrical features (greatest/shortest diameter, equivalent diameter, irregularity index) derived from the ABCD-rule, ignoring color variation which is a key component of the ABCD rule.
